description: Test scenarios where answers are complete/thorough and should trigger PREDEFINED strategy

scenarios.name: Detailed technical implementation

history.text: Describe your experience with distributed training.

I implemented distributed training using PyTorch DDP across 8 A100 GPUs. We used gradient accumulation with a global batch size of 512, implemented overlapped communication to avoid bottlenecks, and used local batch normalization. This reduced training time from 72 hours to 11 hours while maintaining model convergence.

scenarios.reason: Answer provides specific technical details, metrics, and outcomes

scenarios.name: Complete project narrative with challenges

history.text: Tell me about a challenging ML project you worked on.

I built a recommendation engine for our platform that serves 2 million users. The main challenge was cold-start problem for new users. I implemented a hybrid approach combining collaborative filtering with content-based features using user demographics and implicit signals. We used matrix factorization with ALS in PySpark for scalability. After A/B testing, we saw 23% increase in user engagement and 15% increase in conversion rate. The system processes 50K recommendations per second with 99.9% uptime.

scenarios.reason: Comprehensive answer with problem, solution, technologies, and measurable results

scenarios.name: Verified technical specifications

history.text: How have you worked with large datasets?

At SDAIA, I built ETL pipelines using PySpark to process terabytes of structured and unstructured data daily. We ingested data from multiple sources into Hive tables, performed data quality checks with Great Expectations, and built predictive models over billions of records achieving over 80% accuracy. The pipeline handled approximately 5TB of data per day with fault tolerance and automatic recovery mechanisms.

scenarios.reason: Specific quantifiable details with technologies and outcomes

scenarios.name: Detailed architectural decision

history.text: How do you approach model deployment?

I containerize models using Docker with multi-stage builds to minimize image size. For serving, I use FastAPI with async endpoints and implement request batching for throughput optimization. I deploy to Kubernetes with horizontal pod autoscaling based on CPU and custom metrics. For monitoring, I track prediction latency, model drift using Evidently, and business metrics. We use blue-green deployments for zero-downtime updates and maintain model versioning in MLflow for rollback capability.

scenarios.reason: Comprehensive technical approach covering multiple aspects with specific tools

scenarios.name: Complete debugging story with resolution

history.text: Tell me about a time you debugged a difficult production issue.

We had a model serving 99th percentile latency spike from 50ms to 800ms during peak hours. I used distributed tracing with Jaeger and found the bottleneck was in our feature computation layer - specifically, a database query that wasn't using the right index. I added a composite index on user_id and timestamp, implemented query result caching with Redis (5-minute TTL), and added request coalescing for concurrent identical requests. This brought p99 latency down to 45ms even during peak load. I also added dashboards in Grafana to monitor query performance trends.

scenarios.reason: Complete story: problem, investigation approach, root cause, solution, and measured impact

scenarios.name: Detailed methodology with rationale

history.text: How do you handle model evaluation?

For our fraud detection system, accuracy wasn't sufficient because of class imbalance (0.5% fraud rate). I used precision-recall curves and focused on F2-score to prioritize recall over precision, as missing fraud is costlier than false alarms. We implemented stratified k-fold cross-validation with temporal splits to prevent data leakage. For final evaluation, I created a holdout set from the most recent month's data and tracked business metrics: caught fraud value, false positive rate, and manual review workload. We also A/B tested against the previous model in production with 10% traffic for two weeks before full rollout.

scenarios.reason: Demonstrates deep understanding with context-specific choices and business alignment

scenarios.name: Complete LLM fine-tuning workflow

history.text: Walk me through your LLM fine-tuning experience.

I fine-tuned Llama 2-7B for domain-specific legal document analysis. Started with 15K annotated legal documents. Used QLoRA with 4-bit quantization to fit on 4x A100 GPUs, rank-16 adapters, targeting query and value projection matrices. Training took 18 hours with batch size 64, learning rate 2e-4 with cosine schedule, and gradient checkpointing for memory efficiency. Implemented early stopping based on validation perplexity. Post-training, I merged adapters and quantized to INT8 using GPTQ for inference. Final model achieved 89% accuracy on legal clause classification, 15% better than base model, with inference latency under 200ms per document on CPU.

scenarios.reason: Comprehensive technical workflow from data through deployment with specific hyperparameters and results

scenarios.name: Detailed team collaboration example

history.text: How do you work with cross-functional teams?

For our personalization project, I worked closely with product managers, frontend engineers, and data engineers. I held weekly technical design reviews where I explained model behavior using SHAP values to help PMs understand feature importance. Created Jupyter notebooks with interactive visualizations for stakeholder demos. Worked with frontend team to implement client-side feature collection and optimize payload sizes - reduced from 50KB to 8KB. Collaborated with data engineers on the feature store design using Feast, ensuring low-latency feature serving (p95 < 10ms). Set up regular model performance reviews where business metrics were linked back to ML metrics, helping everyone understand the model's business impact.

scenarios.reason: Shows comprehensive collaboration skills with specific examples and measurable outcomes

scenarios.name: Complete RAG implementation details

history.text: You mentioned RAG pipelines on your resume. Tell me about your implementation.

I built a RAG system for internal documentation search. Used sentence-transformers (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) for embeddings, stored in Milvus with HNSW indexing. Implemented hybrid search combining dense retrieval with BM25 sparse retrieval, then reranking top-20 results using cross-encoder. Added metadata filtering for document types and departments. The LLM component used GPT-3.5 with carefully engineered system prompts and few-shot examples. Implemented citation tracking so responses linked back to source documents. Added semantic caching with Redis to avoid redundant LLM calls for similar queries. Final system achieved 85% answer relevance score and reduced support ticket volume by 40%.

scenarios.reason: Exhaustive technical details covering architecture, technologies, optimizations, and business impact

scenarios.name: Thorough data pipeline explanation

history.text: Describe your most complex data pipeline.

Built a real-time feature engineering pipeline processing clickstream data. Used Kafka for ingestion (3M events/min peak), Flink for stream processing with 5-minute tumbling windows for aggregations. Implemented exactly-once semantics using Kafka transactions and Flink checkpoints every 30 seconds. Features included user session metrics, rolling 7-day aggregates, and real-time trend detection. Sank to Redis for online serving (TTL 24h) and S3 for batch model training. Added dead letter queues for malformed events and comprehensive monitoring with Prometheus. Pipeline had 99.95% uptime and end-to-end latency p99 under 500ms.

scenarios.reason: Complete pipeline architecture with scale, reliability patterns, and performance metrics
